content.full_text:
Memorandum

To : Opinion Request Meeting Participants          Date : Feb. 17, 1977

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Mike Baker

Subject: Disclosure of interests in real property.

The following questions have arisen in connection with
the requirement that officials disclose interests in real
property:

(1) Does a holder of a first deed of trust have a
    reportable interest in real property?

(2) Does a holder of a second or a third deed of trust
    have a reportable interest in real property?

(3) Does a seller of real property under a contract of
    sale have a reportable interest in real property?

(4) If the answer to any of the above questions is yes,
    what is the value of that reportable interest?

(5) Assuming that the purchaser of real property has a
    reportable interest in real property, what is the
    value of that interest?

To help us in answering these questions, it is my understanding that real estate transactions typically have the following features:

(1) When a person finances the purchase of real property by
    means of a mortgage or a deed of trust, the purchaser
    takes title to the land. The lender's remedy in the
    event of default is to force a sale of the property.
    Under such an arrangement the lender does not have the
    option of taking title to the land. The proceeds of
    the sale go first to the lender to satisfy the outstanding balance on the note or mortgage, and the excess is
    turned over to the purchaser. Almost all real estate
    transactions today are financed by deeds of trust rather
    than mortgages, even those financed by commercial institutions. The essential difference between a mortgage

Value (comparable
    Trust, D of T - value is amount of debt
    Sales - value of property minus any mortgages &
    Buyer - value of property minus the mortgage

M.J
Feb. 17, 1977

Disclosure of interests in real property.

and a deed of trust is that a mortgage permits a forced
sale only after recourse to judicial proceedings, whereas
a deed of trust does not require a judicial order. Even
so, it is my understanding that most mortgage agreements
have a provision in them that permits a forced sale with-
out the intervention of a court.

(2) The other typical financing technique is a contract of
sale. In such a case, the seller retains the title to the
land and agrees to transfer title to the buyer on full
payment of the purchase price. The most common type of
contract of sale contains a "deed of trust" type of
provision which requires a forced sale of the property
in the event of default by the buyer. In such a case,
therefore, the seller does not have the option of re-
taining title to the land.

(3) A contract of sale can, however, contain a provision which
permits the seller to retain title to the property in the
event of default by the buyer. In such a case, the trend
of the courts is to require that the seller make some sort
of restitution to the buyer as a condition for taking back
the property. There is no fixed rule for determining what
the amount of restitution should be. I am told that a
typical index is the fair market rental value of the
property during the buyer's occupancy.

(4) Deficiency judgments are not permitted in California ex-
cept on commercial property or on apartment units con-
taining four or more units (CCP Section 580(b) and (d)).
The need for seeking a deficiency judgment would arise
if the value of the property has depreciated and a forced
sale does not yield enough to cover the balance due on
the notes.

(5) I am told that it is not unusual for purchasers of com-
mercial or speculative property to obtain 100% or close
to 100% financing. I am also told that it is a typical
financing technique to obtain a second or third mortgage
on commercial or speculative property upon appreciation
of the property to finance other unrelated business pro-
jects. Thus, it may not be particularly unusual for a
purchaser of property not to have a $1,000 interest when
only equity and appreciation is considered. If this is
true, it would be an argument for requiring that the "fair
market value" of an interest in real property for purposes
of Section 82033 be considered the fair market value of
the property, not the value of the equity plus appreciation.

MJB:jo

sections.question:
(1) Does a holder of a first deed of trust have a reportable interest in real property?

(2) Does a holder of a second or a third deed of trust have a reportable interest in real property?

(3) Does a seller of real property under a contract of sale have a reportable interest in real property?

(4) If the answer to any of the above questions is yes, what is the value of that reportable interest?

(5) Assuming that the purchaser of real property has a reportable interest in real property, what is the value of that interest?
sections.question_synthetic: The memorandum addresses five related questions concerning what interests in real property must be disclosed by officials, including whether holders of various deeds of trust, sellers under contracts of sale, and purchasers have reportable interests, and if so, how to value those interests.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC provides background on real estate financing structures (mortgages, deeds of trust, and contracts of sale) and valuation frameworks, suggesting that trust deed values should be measured by the amount of debt, sales interests by property value minus mortgages, and purchaser interests by property value minus the mortgage.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document appears to be an internal FPPC memorandum presenting questions and background analysis rather than a formal final advice letter with clear conclusion section. The valuation guidance at the top of the second page appears to be preliminary working notes. No formal CONCLUSION or SHORT ANSWER section is present, making this a partial response document.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: (1) Does a holder of a first deed of trust have a reportable interest in real property?

(2) Does a holder of a second or a third deed of trust have a reportable interest in real property?

(3) Does a seller of real property under a contract of sale have a reportable interest in real property?

(4) If the answer to any of the above questions is yes, what is the value of that reportable interest?

(5) Assuming that the purchaser of real property has a reportable interest in real property, what is the value of that interest?

CONCLUSION: The FPPC provides background on real estate financing structures (mortgages, deeds of trust, and contracts of sale) and valuation frameworks, suggesting that trust deed values should be measured by the amount of debt, sales interests by property value minus mortgages, and purchaser interests by property value minus the mortgage.

embedding.summary: This February 1977 FPPC memorandum addresses questions about which parties holding interests in real property transactions must disclose those interests and how to value them, providing technical background on real estate financing mechanisms and preliminary valuation guidance.
